claim_text: Platooning could lead to 4.5% fuel savings for the lead truck and 10% fuel savings for the following truck.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references that are directly relevant to the claim. If none are appropriate, return an empty list.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (20171005-bermans-bill-advancing-green-trucking-technologies-signed-law.txt / #5): Platooning could lead to 4.5% fuel savings for the lead truck and 10% fuel savings for the following truck.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Effect of Heavy-Truck Platooning Fuel-Efficiency Gains on Overall Fuel Efficiency
references.abstract: This study examines the impact of platooning fuel-efficiency gains on overall fuel efficiency in heavy-truck operations, considering various travel conditions beyond platooning scenarios.

references.title: Impact of Mixed Traffic on the Energy Savings of a Truck Platoon
references.abstract: This paper investigates the effects of mixed traffic conditions on the energy savings of a two-truck platoon equipped with cooperative adaptive cruise control, highlighting real-world challenges to fuel efficiency.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Class-8 Trucks with Standard-Trailers Net a Fuel Savings of Between 5.2 and 7.8 Percent in a Three-Truck CACC Platoon. With Aerodynamic-Trailers, These Savings Can Grow to 14.2 Percent
references.abstract: This study evaluates the fuel-saving benefits of three-truck platooning using cooperative adaptive cruise control, demonstrating significant improvements with aerodynamic trailers.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Simulation Analysis Estimates Fuel Savings of Up to 12.3 Percent Using Long-Horizon Predictive Cruise Control in Truck Platooning
references.abstract: This paper presents a simulation analysis estimating fuel savings of up to 12.3% using long-horizon predictive cruise control in truck platooning, considering various control strategies and terrain effects.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Vehicle Platooning Impact on Drag Coefficients and Energy/Fuel Saving Implications
references.abstract: This study develops power models to estimate fuel reduction in vehicle platoons, showing significant fuel savings for heavy-duty truck platoons compared to constant drag coefficient assumptions.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Considerate and Cooperative Model Predictive Control for Energy-Efficient Truck Platooning of Heterogeneous Fleets
references.abstract: This paper presents a cooperative model predictive control strategy for energy-efficient truck platooning, demonstrating improved harmonization and fuel economy in real-world driving scenarios.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Reducing Road Vehicle Fuel Consumption by Exploiting Connectivity and Automation: A Literature Survey
references.abstract: This survey examines the potential of connectivity and automation to reduce road vehicle fuel consumption, highlighting the dominant role of platooning in highway settings.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Cyber-Physical Control of Road Freight Transport
references.abstract: This paper reviews how modern information and communication technology supports a cyber-physical transportation system architecture with integrated logistic systems coordinating fleets of trucks traveling together in vehicle platoons, achieving significant fuel savings.
references.venue: SAE International Journal of Commercial Vehicles

references.title: Low-intensity fires mitigate the risk of high-intensity wildfires in California's forests
references.abstract: This study demonstrates that low-intensity fires substantially reduce the risk of future high-intensity fires in California's conifer forests, with protective effects lasting at least six years.

references.title: Twenty-year study confirms California forests are healthier when burned — or thinned
references.abstract: A two-decade experiment in the Sierra Nevada confirms that prescribed burning and restoration thinning effectively reduce catastrophic wildfire risk and enhance forest health.

references.title: Fire, other ravages jeopardize California's prized forests
references.abstract: This article discusses the severe threats to California's forests, including intense wildfires, droughts, and climate change, and the challenges in forest management.

references.title: Investing in the Direct Care Workforce: Lessons From the CalGrows Training and Stipend Initiative
references.abstract: The CalGrows program in California distributed $150 million in grants to organizations providing free training to direct care workers and caregivers, aiming to address low retention and pay. A survey indicated that approximately 90% of participants felt better prepared and more confident in their caregiving roles, with over 95% believing the training improved care quality. Interviews with grant recipients reported improved employee retention due to the training.
references.venue: Journal of the American Medical Directors Association

references.title: Child Care and Early Education Workforce Recruitment and Retention: Insights from a Current Landscape of Strategies
references.abstract: This report examines current strategies to recruit, advance, and sustain the child care and early education workforce, focusing on economic well-being, qualifications, psychological well-being, workplace demands, and system alignment. It highlights that most strategies are in early stages, limited in reach, and lack sufficient evidence of effectiveness.

references.title: Addressing Economic Instability and Workforce Turnover in Early Childhood Education: The Role of Stipends, Wage Supplements, and Professional Development
references.abstract: This literature review examines the effectiveness of stipends, wage supplements, and professional development programs in reducing turnover and improving job satisfaction among early childhood educators. It finds that financial incentives significantly reduce turnover, while professional development fosters job satisfaction and career advancement. However, sustainability concerns remain, particularly with the expiration of temporary funding sources like the American Rescue Plan.
references.venue: Endeavors: Mississippi State Undergraduate Research Journal

references.title: Staffing and Compensation Challenges in Child Care: Statewide Evidence from Pandemic Relief Applications
references.abstract: This study analyzes data from pandemic relief applications to assess staffing and compensation challenges in child care centers. It finds that the pandemic exacerbated existing staffing issues, with many centers reporting unfilled vacancies and difficulties hiring staff. The study suggests that increased compensation and support are necessary to stabilize the workforce.

references.title: The Child Care Sector Is Still Struggling To Hire Workers
references.abstract: This article discusses the ongoing challenges in the child care sector related to hiring and retaining staff, emphasizing the need for increased compensation and support to address these issues. It highlights that without creating good jobs, the sector will continue to struggle with staffing shortages.

references.abstract: Future projections show that by 2100, global mean sea level is very likely to rise by 1.6–4.3 feet (0.5–1.3 m) under the higher scenario (RCP8.5), 1.1–3.1 feet (0.35–0.95 m) under a lower scenario (RCP4.5), and 0.8–2.6 feet (0.24–0.79 m) under an even lower scenario (RCP2.6).

references.title: PROJECTED
references.abstract: By 2100, projections for all scenarios indicate a high likelihood (~50% probability or greater) of at least 1.5 feet of sea level rise, with the potential for more than 8 feet at the upper end of the distribution.

references.title: Coastal Effects
references.abstract: In 2100, most of the Atlantic coast of the contiguous US will see rises of 3 to 5 feet, with areas of 5 to 6 feet of rise along the western Gulf Coast. California shows rises of 3 to 4 feet.

references.title: Chapter 12: Sea Level Rise
references.abstract: Global mean sea level is very likely to rise by 0.3–0.6 feet (9–18 cm) by 2030, 0.5–1.2 feet (15–38 cm) by 2050, and 1.0–4.3 feet (30–130 cm) by 2100.

references.title: Sea Level Rise
references.abstract: Global sea level has risen by about 8 inches since reliable record keeping began in 1880. It is projected to rise another 1 to 4 feet by 2100.

references.title: Exploring the effects of health behaviors and mental health on students’ academic achievement: a cross-sectional study on lebanese university students
references.abstract: This study investigates the impact of dietary habits, lifestyle, and mental health on academic success among Lebanese university students, finding that healthier habits and better mental health are associated with higher academic achievement.

references.title: Food Insecurity, Well-being, and Academic Success among College Students: Implications for Post COVID-19 Pandemic Programming
references.abstract: This study assesses the prevalence of food insecurity among college students and its association with mental and physical health, as well as academic success, highlighting the need for targeted interventions to address these issues.

references.title: Exploring housing insecurity in relation to student success
references.abstract: This study explores the relationship between housing instability and academic and mental health outcomes among college students, finding that housing insecurity is associated with lower GPA and poorer mental health.

references.title: College student sleep quality and mental and physical health are associated with food insecurity in a multi-campus study
references.abstract: This study examines the relationship between food insecurity, sleep quality, and health among college students, finding that food-insecure students report poorer sleep quality and more days with mental and physical health issues.

references.title: Prevalence and correlates of food insecurity among U.S. college students: a multi-institutional study
references.abstract: This study assesses the prevalence of food insecurity among U.S. college students and its association with health and academic outcomes, highlighting the need for institutional interventions to address this issue.

references.title: Food insecurity, psychosocial health and academic performance among college and university students in Georgia, USA
references.abstract: This study examines the associations among food insecurity, psychosocial health, and academic performance among college students, finding that food insecurity is associated with poorer psychosocial health and academic performance.

references.title: The effects of inadequate food, housing, and health care on student attrition
references.abstract: This study highlights the compounded effects of unmet needs for housing, food, health care, and mental health treatment on students' academic success and persistence to graduation, emphasizing the need for targeted support mechanisms.

references.title: MEETING BASIC NEEDS TO SUPPORT STUDENTS’ MENTAL HEALTH AND SUCCESS
references.abstract: This fact sheet discusses how unmet basic needs, such as food and housing insecurity, can negatively impact students' mental health and academic success, and emphasizes the importance of addressing these issues to support student well-being.
references.venue: California Community Colleges Student Mental Health Program

references.title: Electric Bicycles and Cargo Bikes—Tools for Parents To Keep On Biking in Auto-Centric Communities? Findings From a US Metropolitan Area
references.abstract: This study presents findings from in-depth interviews with 20 parents and caretakers in the San Francisco Bay Area, revealing that e-bikes, including electrified cargo bikes, are a viable alternative for transporting children, increasing community accessibility, and substituting car trips.

references.title: E-cargo bikes can replace car trips and reshape family travel
references.abstract: Research indicates that electric cargo bikes can replace many car journeys, including school runs and shopping trips, and have the potential to shift how families and communities perceive cycling, making it a more practical and inclusive everyday option.

references.title: Transporting kids by e-bike
references.abstract: This article discusses various electric cargo bikes suitable for transporting children, highlighting models like the Cube Trike Family Hybrid 750 and the Giant Explore E+ 1 GTS, emphasizing their stability, storage capacity, and child-friendly features.

references.title: Understanding Factors That Lead to Electric Bicycles for Bay Area Users with Children
references.abstract: This study focuses on family users of electric bikes, including electrified cargo bikes, to learn how e-bikes are substituted for a family car, through semi-structured interviews of 20 San Francisco Bay Area e-bicyclists.

references.title: Physical activity when riding an electric-assisted bicycle with and without cargo
references.abstract: This study compares time spent riding and exercise intensity when riding an electric-assisted bicycle with cargo (30 kg) and without cargo, and driving a car, finding that e-cargo bikes can be a substitute for cars while providing physical activity benefits.

query:

Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (20210405-boerner-horvaths-bill-incentivize-electric-bicycle-purchases-passes.txt / #6): Eighty percent of vehicle trips in California are less than ten miles.

references.title: Using Connected Vehicle Data to Evaluate National Trip Trends
references.abstract: This study analyzes connected vehicle data to assess national trip trends, including daily mean trip lengths across various states, with California's average trip length being 7.08 miles for HVs, 6.91 miles for ICEVs, and 6.47 miles for EVs.

references.title: Vehicle Trips and Mileage by Trip Length
references.abstract: According to the 2009 National Household Travel Survey, 63% of daily vehicle trips are between one and nine miles in length, indicating that a majority of vehicle trips are relatively short.

references.title: Vehicle access and falling transit ridership: evidence from Southern California
references.abstract: This study examines the relationship between increased private vehicle access and declining transit ridership in Southern California, highlighting the impact of vehicle access on transportation choices.

references.title: Revisiting Average Trip Length Defaults and Adjustment Factors for Quantifying VMT Reductions from Car Share, Bike Share, and Scooter Share Services
references.abstract: This report examines average trip lengths and adjustment factors for shared mobility services, offering insights into how these services impact vehicle miles traveled and transportation behavior.

references.title: California E-Bike Incentive Project
references.abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project provides up to $2,000 in voucher incentives to low-income California residents for the purchase of electric bikes, including cargo and adaptive e-bikes. The project prioritizes funding for low-income Californians and those who live in disadvantaged and low-income communities. It also commits a portion of the funding to e-bike safety and education training, which is part of the application process before purchasing an e-bike.

references.title: E-Bike Purchase Incentives
references.abstract: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is running a multi-year program to offer e-bike purchase incentives of up to $2,000 for qualifying low-income Californians. To qualify, you must earn less than 300% of the 2024 federal poverty level, be over 18, and live in California. For more details about the California program, see our FAQs below or visit the program website: ebikeincentives.org.

references.title: General FAQ's - California E-Bike Incentive Project
references.abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project is a statewide consumer-facing incentive program that aims to provide point-of-sale voucher incentives for the purchase of electric bicycles (e-bikes) to residents in disadvantaged communities, low-income communities, and low-income households with an annual household income of less than 300% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). Eligible applicants can receive up to $2,000 in incentives.

references.title: Retailer FAQs - California E-Bike Incentive Project
references.abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project is a statewide consumer-facing incentive program that aims to provide point-of-sale voucher incentives for the purchase of electric bicycles (e-bikes) to residents in disadvantaged communities, low-income communities, and low-income households with an annual household income of less than 300% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). All California-based e-bike retailers/brands are welcome to apply to take part in the program.

references.title: E-bike program will give 1,000 Californians vouchers to purchase electric bikes on May 29
references.abstract: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is offering $2 million or approximately 1,000 vouchers to eligible applicants who will be selected at random to help ensure a fair distribution of vouchers. The project will continue to collaborate and partner with more than 20 community-based organizations statewide. These organizations will provide outreach and application assistance to low-income residents across California to empower and inform hard-to-reach applicants who encounter additional barriers to program participation such as lack of access to a computer or high-speed internet.

references.title: California puts the brakes on E-Bike vouchers, transfers $18M
references.abstract: The California Air Resources Board said it has ended, for now, a popular program that provides vouchers for low-income Californians to purchase electric bicycles. Instead, about $18 million from the California E-Bike Incentive Project is being redirected to another incentive program called Clean Cars 4 All that helps people trade in their gas-guzzling cars for electric or hybrid vehicles, CARB confirmed. CARB said the e-bike incentive program, which launched in 2024, has helped more than 2,100 Californians with low incomes purchase an e-bike with up to $2,000 in vouchers. The vouchers were doled out to eligible recipients during two application periods.

references.title: New Wheels, Less Cash: California’s E-Bike Vouchers Are Back
references.abstract: California is making it easier for lower-income residents to purchase e-bikes by offering up to $2,000 in point-of-sale incentives. The program is focused on equity, aiming to make e-bikes accessible to those who need them most. Eligible applicants must be 18 or older, live in California, and meet certain income limits. Priority is given to people in low-income and disadvantaged communities. The next application window opens April 29, 2025.

references.title: Just Launched: California E-Bike Incentive Program
references.abstract: The California E-Bike Incentive Project has officially launched, providing up to $2,000 of point-of-sale incentives to support the purchase of a new electric bicycle. This initial phase will allow for the purchase of 1,500 e-bikes across California on a first-come, first-serve basis, and the program is set to expand later in 2025. Income-qualified approved applicants will receive a base voucher of $1,750 and an additional $250 for accessories, which can be applied when purchasing an e-bike, meaning no upfront costs for recipients.

claim_text: Law enforcement agencies in California routinely fail to adequately identify, report, or respond to hate crimes.
query:
Task: Provide up to 25 scientific references that are directly relevant to the claim. If none are appropriate, return an empty list.
Context: California legislative press release analysis.
Claim (20210601-amid-rising-violence-state-assembly-passes-legislation-strengthen-hate.txt / #3): Law enforcement agencies in California routinely fail to adequately identify, report, or respond to hate crimes.
Constraints:
- Prioritize peer-reviewed sources.
- Include DOI when available.
- Keep abstracts to 1-2 sentence summaries if exact abstracts not available.
- Prefer recency for fast-moving topics (e.g., deepfakes, LLMs), else include seminal works.
- provider = discovery source (e.g., 'arXiv' if link is on arxiv.org; 'publisher' for publisher site);
- venue = actual journal or conference (use 'arXiv' only if there is no published venue).
- Normalize venue names to standard short forms (e.g., NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CVPR, ICCV, ECCV, AAAI, IJCAI, ACL, EMNLP, NAACL).
Output: JSON object {"references": [{provider,id,doi,title,abstract,year,venue,authors,url,is_open_access}...]}.

references.title: Police Responses to Hate Crimes: A Review of the Literature
references.abstract: This review explores the challenges and inadequacies in police responses to hate crimes, highlighting systemic failures in the identification and reporting processes.

references.title: The Role of Police in Addressing Hate Crimes
references.abstract: This article analyzes the effectiveness of police agencies in handling hate crimes and the impact of their reporting practices on community trust.

references.title: An Analysis of Hate Crime Reporting in the United States
references.abstract: This study examines how law enforcement agencies report and categorize hate crimes, highlighting significant gaps in data collection and reporting accuracy.

references.title: Understanding Law Enforcement’s Response to Hate Crimes: Impacts on Victims and Communities
references.abstract: This article critiques law enforcement's response to hate crimes, discussing the implications for victims and the broader community response.

references.title: Police as a Social Control Mechanism: An Examination of Hate Crimes
references.abstract: This paper discusses the role of police in the societal context of hate crimes, addressing the failures in adequate response and reporting.

references.abstract: Employers in California are required to protect workers from workplace hazards, including unhealthy air from wildfire smoke, by monitoring air quality and implementing protective measures.

references.title: The origins of California’s gun violence restraining order law: a case study using Kingdon’s multiple streams framework
references.abstract: This study examines the passage of California's Gun Violence Restraining Order (GVRO) law through the lens of Kingdon's multiple streams framework, highlighting the bipartisan collaboration and policy entrepreneurship involved in its enactment.

references.title: Firearm Violence Following the Implementation of California’s Gun Violence Restraining Order Law
references.abstract: This study evaluates the impact of California's GVRO law on firearm violence, finding no significant association between its implementation and reductions in firearm assault or self-harm at the population level in San Diego.

references.title: Implementation and perceived effectiveness of gun violence restraining orders in California: A qualitative evaluation
references.abstract: This qualitative evaluation explores the implementation and perceived effectiveness of GVROs in California, highlighting the law's bipartisan support and its role in preventing firearm violence, particularly suicides.

references.title: City of Fremont Announces Opening of Housing Navigation Center for Homeless Individuals
references.abstract: The City of Fremont announced the opening of its Housing Navigation Center (HNC) on August 31, 2020, aiming to provide shelter and a path to permanent housing for unhoused community members. The center began housing residents in September 2020, offering services for up to six months, with plans to operate for three years, potentially extending to five years based on funding. The initiative received funding from state, county, and city programs, totaling $9.47 million over the first three years.

references.title: Fremont Housing Navigation Center Shares Successful Six-Month Update
references.abstract: In its first six months of operation, the Fremont Housing Navigation Center (HNC) served 44 individuals, with 21 transitioning into permanent housing. The center operated under reduced capacity due to COVID-19, housing 25 residents on average for four months. The initiative received monetary donations totaling $40,346 and developed connections with 19 local landlords. The HNC is funded by various state and county programs, with plans to increase occupancy as COVID-19 cases decrease.

references.title: Fremont Housing Navigation Center Shares Successful Second Year Update
references.abstract: In its second year of operation, the Fremont Housing Navigation Center (HNC) provided housing for 83 unhoused individuals, assisting 46 residents in finding stable housing and 22 in obtaining employment upon exiting the program. The center operated under budget at $2.01 million, fully funded by state and county sources, including the State Permanent Local Housing Allocation (PLHA) and Alameda County Social Service Agency Funding.

references.title: Fremont Housing Navigation Center Marks Four Years of Impact
references.abstract: The Fremont Housing Navigation Center (HNC) completed its fourth year of operation, housing 233 local individuals since its opening in the fall of 2020. In partnership with Bay Area Community Services (BACS), the center assisted 108 residents in finding stable housing and 55 individuals in obtaining employment. The HNC operated under budget at $2.01 million, fully funded by state and county sources, including the State Permanent Local Housing Allocation (PLHA) and Alameda County Social Service Agency Funding.

references.title: Fremont opens 1st homeless navigation center, following example set in San Francisco and Oakland
references.abstract: The City of Fremont opened its first homeless navigation center, designed to house about 45 residents, in a parking lot behind Fremont City Hall. Due to COVID-19 health and safety protocols, the center initially operated at a reduced capacity of 25 residents. The facility began housing residents in September 2020, offering services for up to six months, with plans to operate for up to three years, potentially extending to five years based on funding. The center aimed to provide shelter and a path to permanent housing for unhoused community members.

references.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Mental Health in Adolescents: A Systematic Review
references.abstract: This systematic review evaluates the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on adolescent mental health, finding increased rates of anxiety, depression, and stress globally, with social support and positive coping skills mitigating these effects.
references.venue: Int. J. Environ. Res. Public Health
authors: Jackson State University School of Public Health

references.title: Adverse Childhood Experiences During the COVID-19 Pandemic and Associations with Poor Mental Health and Suicidal Behaviors Among High School Students — Adolescent Behaviors and Experiences Survey, United States, January–June 2021
references.abstract: This study found that high school students reporting four or more adverse childhood experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic had significantly higher rates of poor mental health and suicidal behaviors, highlighting the need for targeted interventions.

references.title: Social factors associated with self-reported changes in mental health symptoms among youth in the COVID-19 pandemic: a cross-sectional survey
references.abstract: This survey found that over one-third of children and youth reported worsened mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic, with changes in familial relationships and school closures being significant contributing factors.

references.title: Exploring the psychological impact on children and adolescents during the initial period of the COVID-19 pandemic—a systematic review
references.abstract: This review examines the risk and protective factors linked to COVID-19 and how they affect the mental health of adolescents, highlighting increased rates of depression and anxiety during the pandemic.

references.title: Adolescent and young adult stress and coping during COVID-19: the utility of a pediatric emergency department screener
references.abstract: This study assesses stress and coping mechanisms among adolescents and young adults during the COVID-19 pandemic, emphasizing the utility of pediatric emergency department screeners in identifying mental health needs.

references.title: Optimal Management of a Smart Port with Shore-Connection and Hydrogen Supplying by Stochastic Model Predictive Control
references.abstract: This paper proposes an optimal management strategy for a Smart Port equipped with renewable generation, including an electrified quay operating Cold-Ironing and a Hydrogen-based quay supplying Zero-Emission Ships. The strategy utilizes Battery Energy Storage and Hydrogen Energy Storage Systems to manage renewable energy sources and supply electric and hydrogen-fueled ships. A model predictive control algorithm is designed to define the best economic strategy during operations, considering uncertainties in renewable energy generation through stochastic optimization. The approach is tested on a potential future Smart Port equipped with wind and photovoltaic generation.

references.title: Artificial Intelligence in Port Logistics: A Bibliometric Analysis of Technological Integration and Research Dynamics
references.abstract: This paper explores the transformation of port logistics operations with artificial intelligence during the port transformation into a smart port. The research integrates capabilities-based resource analysis and dynamic capabilities with sociotechnical implementations of technologies and resilience approaches of complex systems under disruptions. The system applies robust data infrastructures to propel analytical and AI modules that become effective once integrated with sufficient governance systems and trained personnel and operational processes to transform planning and safety and sustainability operations. It applies Scopus bibliometric research to analyze 123 articles using a systematic approach with both a search protocol and a document screening and duplication verification. It incorporates annual behavior and distribution of author and country performance analysis with science mapping techniques that explore keyword relation and co-citation and bibliographic coupling and conceptual structuring tools that construct thematic maps and multiple correspondence analysis with community detection while applying explicit thresholding and robust tests. The research connects AI applications to smart port domains through specific data-to-impact pathways while providing a method for bibliometric analysis that enables future updates. The research presents a step-by-step approach for data readiness followed by predictive and optimization implementation and organizational integration. The paper supports public policy through recommendations for data sharing standards and complete environmental benefit assessments. The research proposes a future study plan which combines field-based testing with multiple port assessments to enhance both cause-effect understanding and research applicability.

references.title: Reducing carbon emissions in the dry port-seaport system: a shared transport approach
references.abstract: This research analyses the carbon emissions associated with a shared transport method within the dry port-seaport network, focusing on the realm of green transportation. Utilizing theoretical analysis and numerical examples, the study contrasts carbon emission levels across three distinct scenarios: shared transport, non-shared transport, and direct road transport. Numerical investigations are conducted to assess the environmental value sensitivity. Results indicate that when the total distance traveled by shippers exceeds that of shared transport, the milk-run transport approach can markedly decrease carbon emissions. Furthermore, transitioning from unimodal road transport to intermodal transport has the potential to yield significant carbon emission savings. This study offers a validated policy objective for fostering a more sustainable transportation system by promoting a modal shift from direct to shared transport.

references.title: Green innovation in ports: drivers, domains, and challenges
references.abstract: The transition toward sustainable maritime development has placed ports at the forefront of global green transformation. As critical nodes in maritime supply chains, ports are under growing pressure to reduce emissions, optimize resource use, and adopt digital technologies. While numerous green practices have emerged in recent years, the literature on port green innovation remains fragmented, lacking a comprehensive synthesis of its drivers, application domains, and systemic challenges. To address this gap, this study conducts a systematic review guided by the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) framework, drawing on peer-reviewed publications from the Scopus and Web of Science databases. The analysis identifies three principal drivers of green innovation in ports: internal dynamics stemming from economic and technological capabilities, external pressures related to environmental regulation and policy, and societal expectations that steer transformation. Green innovation practices are classified into four functional domains: foundational measures, operational optimization, energy system transformation, and intelligent upgrading. In parallel, four major challenges are revealed: persistent ecological pressures, energy transition bottlenecks, fragmented regulatory frameworks, and operational trade-offs. This review contributes an integrated analytical framework that clarifies the conceptual landscape of port green innovation and offers actionable insights for research, policy, and strategic planning.

references.title: Roadmap to a zero-emission port: A case study in Port of Yangpu
references.abstract: This briefing addresses the urgent need to decarbonize ports, focusing on the Port of Yangpu in Hainan, China. Despite China being home to seven of the top 10 global container ports, the paper highlights a lack of climate ambitions among these ports. The Port of Yangpu, anticipating significant growth in ship traffic, is presented with an opportunity to develop a decarbonization strategy. The analysis outlines three scenarios—Announced Ambitions, Partial Decarbonization, and Full Decarbonization—to assess technological roadmaps for achieving zero well-to-wake (WTW) carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e) emissions by 2050. While the Announced Ambitions scenario falls short, the more aggressive Partial and Full Decarbonization scenarios, relying on electrification and zero-emission fuels, offer potential solutions, with the Full Decarbonization scenario achieving zero emissions by 2050. The case study acknowledges varying costs associated with these technological pathways and suggests that economic benefits and environmental advantages could outweigh these costs. The briefing emphasizes integrating decarbonization plans into development strategies, refining roadmaps based on cost-effectiveness, and early planning for achieving decarbonization goals. The analysis stresses the necessity for the Port of Yangpu to adopt electrification and zero-emission fuels to meet ambitious decarbonization goals by 2050 and cautions against relying on fossil liquified natural gas as a bridging fuel solution given the issue of methane slip with marine engines.

references.title: New Funding to Help More Than 2,600 Californians Move From Encampments Into Housing
references.abstract: California is awarding more than $81 million to fund 12 projects to move unhoused Californians from encampments into housing. The Newsom Administration has committed more than $30 billion to address housing and homelessness since taking office, with $3.5 billion for homelessness in the 2023 state budget alone.

references.title: Governor’s 2023-24 Budget Continues to Address Housing and Homelessness
references.abstract: The Governor’s budget includes $3.4 billion in Fiscal Year 2023-24 to prevent and end homelessness, including $1 billion for a fifth round of Homeless Housing, Assistance and Prevention (HHAP) grants, and $400 million for a third round of Encampment Resolution Fund grants.

references.title: Governor’s Proposed State Budget
references.abstract: The budget earmarks $3.4 billion from the General Fund FY 2023-24 for programs within the Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD), the California Interagency Council on Homelessness (Cal ICH), and the California Tax Credit Allocation Committee, FY 2023-24. This funding includes $400 million for a third round of encampment resolution grants.
references.venue: California Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation

references.title: Governor Awards $130.7 Million to Help 18 California Communities House People Living in Encampments
references.abstract: Governor Gavin Newsom announced nearly $130.7 million in Encampment Resolution Funding (ERF) program grants to help 18 cities, counties, and Continuums of Care (CoCs) resolve critical encampment concerns and address the housing and health and safety needs of 3,364 people living in encampments, and permanently house 1,565 people.
references.venue: California Department of Housing and Community Development

references.title: Governor Newsom Announces $1 Billion in Homelessness Funding, Launches State’s Largest Mobilization of Small Homes
references.abstract: Governor Newsom announced the release of $1 billion in Homeless Housing, Assistance and Prevention (HHAP) Round 4 funding to support communities across the state stepping up their work to reduce homelessness. The state is delivering 1,200 small homes to Los Angeles, San Diego County, San Jose, and Sacramento to provide safe, interim housing for people experiencing homelessness.

references.title: California announces funding to create thousands of affordable homes for California families
references.abstract: The Governor’s strategy is producing real results throughout the state. California is making strong progress in reducing homelessness in communities throughout California, with many communities reporting through the state’s support, more people are getting help than ever, and their homelessness rates continue to drop.

references.title: CPUC Opens Public Inquiry on High Winter Natural Gas Prices
references.abstract: The California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) initiated an investigation into the causes and impacts of the winter 2022-2023 natural gas price spikes, examining potential recurrence, effects on customer bills, and reliability threats.

references.title: Governor Newsom Calls for Federal Investigation of High Natural Gas Prices As California Provides Relief
references.abstract: Governor Gavin Newsom urged the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to investigate potential market manipulation and anticompetitive behavior contributing to high natural gas prices in California, while announcing state-provided relief measures.

references.title: Rep. Levin Calls for Investigation into Natural Gas Price Hikes
references.abstract: U.S. Representative Mike Levin urged the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to investigate the causes of significant price volatility in California's natural gas market, highlighting steep price hikes despite national prices remaining low.

references.title: California regulators look into natural gas price spikes, power market impacts
references.abstract: California regulators, including the CPUC and California Energy Commission, are investigating the causes of recent natural gas price spikes and their impact on power markets, with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) being urged to examine potential market manipulation.

references.title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Lack of Adherence to Medical and Ethical Practice Standards; A Joint Position Statement of the Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine and the North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology
references.abstract: Crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) attempt to dissuade pregnant people from considering abortion, often using misinformation and unethical practices. While mimicking health care clinics, CPCs provide biased, limited, and inaccurate health information, including incomplete pregnancy options counseling and unscientific sexual and reproductive health information. The centers do not provide or refer for abortion or contraception but often advertise in ways that give the appearance that they do provide these services without disclosing the biased nature and marked limitations of their services. Although individuals working in CPCs in the United States have First Amendment rights to free speech, their provision of misinformation might be harmful to young people and adults. The Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine and North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology support the following positions: (1) CPCs pose risk by failing to adhere to medical and ethical practice standards; (2) governments should only support health programs that provide accurate, comprehensive information; (3) CPCs and individuals who provide CPC services should be held to established standards of ethics and medical care; (4) schools should not outsource sexual education to CPCs or other entities that do not provide accurate and complete health information; (5) search engines and digital platforms should enforce policies against misleading advertising by CPCs; and (6) health professionals should educate themselves, and young people about CPCs and help young people identify safe, quality sources of sexual and reproductive health information and care.
authors: Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine
North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology

references.title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Post-Dobbs Characteristics and Changes
references.abstract: This study enumerates crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) in the United States in 2024 and examines changes before and after the Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization decision. The study found that in 2024, 2,633 CPCs operated in the United States, a 3.3% increase from 2021. Nationally, 71.7% offered nondiagnostic ultrasounds, 28.0% advertised sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing, 17.2% STI treatment, 6.8% HIV testing, and 3.8% unproven 'abortion reversal' on-site. The total count of CPCs in 2024 was 230% greater in states with a grant program, and the rate of newly identified and re-opened CPCs was 1.81 times greater, adjusting for CPCs in 2021.

references.title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers Offer Dubious Advice on 'Abortion Pill Reversal'
references.abstract: This study found that nearly a third (30%) of crisis pregnancy centers promote 'abortion pill reversal,' an unproven and potentially dangerous intervention. The study highlights that this treatment lacks FDA approval and could increase the risk of serious complications like sepsis, especially when it interferes with the completion of a medication abortion.

references.title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Lack of Adherence to Medical and Ethical Practice Standards
references.abstract: Crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) attempt to dissuade pregnant people from considering abortion, often using misinformation and unethical practices. While mimicking health care clinics, CPCs provide biased, limited, and inaccurate health information, including incomplete pregnancy options counseling and unscientific sexual and reproductive health information. The centers do not provide or refer for abortion or contraception but often advertise in ways that give the appearance that they do provide these services without disclosing the biased nature and marked limitations of their services. Although individuals working in CPCs in the United States have First Amendment rights to free speech, their provision of misinformation might be harmful to young people and adults. The Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine and North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology support the following positions: (1) CPCs pose risk by failing to adhere to medical and ethical practice standards; (2) governments should only support health programs that provide accurate, comprehensive information; (3) CPCs and individuals who provide CPC services should be held to established standards of ethics and medical care; (4) schools should not outsource sexual education to CPCs or other entities that do not provide accurate and complete health information; (5) search engines and digital platforms should enforce policies against misleading advertising by CPCs; and (6) health professionals should educate themselves, and young people about CPCs and help young people identify safe, quality sources of sexual and reproductive health information and care.
authors: Society for Adolescent Health and Medicine
North American Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Gynecology

references.title: Crisis Pregnancy Centers in the United States: Post-Dobbs Characteristics and Changes
references.abstract: This study enumerates crisis pregnancy centers (CPCs) in the United States in 2024 and examines changes before and after the Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization decision. The study found that in 2024, 2,633 CPCs operated in the United States, a 3.3% increase from 2021. Nationally, 71.7% offered nondiagnostic ultrasounds, 28.0% advertised sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing, 17.2% STI treatment, 6.8% HIV testing, and 3.8% unproven 'abortion reversal' on-site. The total count of CPCs in 2024 was 230% greater in states with a grant program, and the rate of newly identified and re-opened CPCs was 1.81 times greater, adjusting for CPCs in 2021.

references.abstract: The California Energy Commission has established offshore wind planning goals of 2 to 5 gigawatts by 2030 and 25 gigawatts by 2045, as part of its strategic plan for offshore wind energy development.

references.title: California Joins Global Offshore Wind Alliance in Ambitious Commitment
references.abstract: At the COP 28 climate conference in Dubai, California Energy Commission Chair David Hochschild announced that California has joined the Global Offshore Wind Alliance, aiming to build 380 gigawatts of offshore wind energy by 2030, with California's own target of 25 gigawatts by 2045.

references.title: California Continues to Advance Offshore Wind With New Report Detailing Options for Permitting Projects
references.abstract: The California Energy Commission adopted a report outlining options for a coordinated, comprehensive, and efficient permitting and environmental review process for offshore wind projects off the California coast, supporting the state's goal of 25,000 MW by 2045.

references.title: Offshore Wind
references.abstract: The California Ocean Protection Council supports the implementation of the Assembly Bill 525 Offshore Wind Energy Strategic Plan, developed by the California Energy Commission, to guide the development of offshore wind energy, with a goal of 5 GW by 2030 and 25 GW by 2045.

references.title: A Strong Start to California's Offshore Wind Plans
references.abstract: A report by the California Energy Commission finds that California has the potential to build almost 25 GW of offshore wind, enough to power more than 25 million homes in PG&E territory by 2045, with 5 GW of this amount potentially built by 2030.

references.title: Energy Commission Sets California Offshore Wind Target of 25 GW by 2045
references.abstract: The California Energy Commission adopted a report establishing offshore wind planning targets of 2-5 gigawatts by 2030 and 25 gigawatts by 2045, following Governor Gavin Newsom’s call for a planning goal of at least 20 GW by 2045.

references.title: California Raises 2030 Offshore Wind Goal, Targets 25 GW by 2045
references.abstract: The California Energy Commission expanded the state’s offshore wind planning goals from 3 GW to 2-5 GW by 2030, with an aim to have up to 25 GW installed by 2045, following Governor Gavin Newsom’s call for the increase.

references.title: Improving California’s Vote-by-Mail Process
references.abstract: This report examines vote-by-mail ballot rejection rates in California, highlighting that 1.7% of ballots were rejected over the past decade, with young and newly registered voters experiencing higher rejection rates. The study identifies late arrival and signature issues as leading causes of rejection.

references.title: Report finds 1.7 percent of California vote-by-mail ballots rejected on average
references.abstract: A report by the California Voter Foundation reveals that, on average, 1.7% of vote-by-mail ballots in California were rejected over the past decade. The study found that young and newly registered voters had significantly higher rejection rates, with late arrival and signature issues being the primary causes.

references.title: USC report finds voters underutilizing free tool to track status of vote-by-mail ballots
references.abstract: A study by the USC Price School’s Center for Inclusive Democracy found that a significant number of California voters are not using or are not aware of BallotTrax, a free tool adopted by all counties during the 2020 election cycle to help individuals track the status of their vote-by-mail ballot.
references.venue: USC Price School’s Center for Inclusive Democracy
authors: USC Price School’s Center for Inclusive Democracy

references.title: Senate committee examines California vote-by-mail signature "cure" process amid disparities and county variation
references.abstract: A California Senate committee examined the state's vote-by-mail signature curing process, highlighting disparities and variations across counties. The committee discussed how local procedures affect outcomes and emphasized the need for uniformity in the curing process to ensure every vote counts.

references.abstract: This study highlights the need for adaptive strategies in the nursing workforce to respond to evolving healthcare requirements, emphasizing the importance of expanding nursing training programs to address workforce challenges in underserved areas.

references.title: Causes and effects of hospital nursing shortages to consider potential feedback effects: an umbrella review
references.abstract: This umbrella review identifies causes and effects of hospital nursing shortages, suggesting that shortages may be self-reinforcing, and emphasizes the need for early intervention and expansion of nursing training programs to address workforce challenges.

references.title: Factors affecting nurses retention during the COVID‐19 pandemic: a systematic review

references.title: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in drinking water in Southeast Los Angeles: Industrial legacy and environmental justice
references.abstract: This study found that 30% of tap water samples in Southeast Los Angeles contained PFAS, with 89% of detections occurring in heavily industrialized areas. Concentrations of PFOA and PFOS exceeded state and national health-based goals, highlighting the need for systematic testing and improved water treatments in socioeconomically disadvantaged communities.

references.title: Mitigating PFAS contamination in the United States: assessing the impact of California's legislation from 2018 to 2022 on drinking water quality
references.abstract: This study evaluated the impact of California's PFAS legislation on PFOA and PFOS contamination levels in public drinking water. A significant decline in both contaminants was observed post-legislation, suggesting that robust state-level regulatory measures can effectively reduce PFAS contamination.

references.title: Bayesian Variable Selection for Censored Spatial Responses with Application to PFAS Concentrations in California
references.abstract: This study presents a Bayesian hierarchical framework to analyze PFAS concentrations in California groundwater, identifying key predictors such as demographic composition, industrial facility counts, proximity to airports, traffic density, and environmental features. The findings offer actionable insights into the environmental and industrial factors affecting PFAS concentrations.

references.title: Drinking water contamination as a population-wide determinant of mortality in California
references.abstract: This large-scale data analysis of 20 million water samples from 2012-2022 in California assesses the impact of drinking water quality on all-cause mortality. The study found that surfactants, potentially proxies for wastewater contaminants, were associated with increased mortality, highlighting the need for public health efforts to mitigate risks associated with contaminated drinking water.

references.abstract: A systematic review found that exposure to certain chemicals, including organohalogenated compounds and phthalates, is associated with earlier breast development in girls.

references.title: USDA Announces Availability of $1.13 Billion for Local Food Programs
references.abstract: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced a $1.13 billion investment to support local and regional food systems, building upon previous investments in the Local Food Purchase Assistance Cooperative Agreement (LFPA) and Local Food for Schools (LFS) programs. This funding aims to help states, territories, and Tribes purchase wholesome, locally produced foods for distribution within their communities to emergency food providers, schools, and child care centers.

references.title: USDA Invests $1.7 Billion to Support Farmers, Deliver Nutrition Assistance, and Bolster Rural Economies
references.abstract: The U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced an investment of $1.7 billion for the purchase of locally and regionally produced foods and domestically produced foods for emergency food assistance. These investments aim to help American producers by ensuring they have access to local and regional markets and to provide American children and families in need with healthy food at schools, child care facilities, and emergency food providers.

references.title: Governor Hochul Announces $10 Million to Expand Access to Food for All New Yorkers
references.abstract: Governor Kathy Hochul announced $10 million through the State’s Food Access Expansion Grant Program to increase food access for New Yorkers living in areas with limited options for affordable, fresh food. The program provides funding to nine organizations across the state to support the development and expansion of supermarkets, food cooperatives, permanent farm stands, mobile markets, and other retail food stores in underserved regions while also increasing markets for New York farmers.
references.venue: New York State Department of Agriculture and Markets

references.title: Shapiro Administration Announces $21 Million Investment to Strengthen Pennsylvania’s Food Supply Chain and Grow the Commonwealth’s Agricultural Industry
references.abstract: The Shapiro Administration announced over $21 million in federally-funded grants to 77 farms and food manufacturers to increase capacity, drive growth, and provide opportunity throughout Pennsylvania’s food supply chain. The funding comes through Pennsylvania’s Resilient Food Systems Infrastructure (RFSI) program, a cooperative agreement with the U.S. Department of Agriculture, which provides dollar-for-dollar matching grants to support food processing, distribution, and aggregation from multiple farms to help meet demand for local, seasonal, and fresh food.

references.title: State Funding Enables Rural Grocers to Expand Florida-Grown Produce Supply
references.abstract: Feeding Florida’s program, Fresh Access Bucks, added 10 new grocery partners with locations that primarily serve rural Florida communities. The Florida Legislature funded Feeding Florida’s $1 million appropriations request for this program expansion focused on connecting customers at Florida’s rural grocers with access to more fresh fruits and vegetables, where affordable, local produce is often difficult to access.

references.abstract: This study introduces SnuggleSense, a system designed to empower survivors of online interpersonal harm by guiding them through a structured sensemaking process, significantly enhancing their understanding and coping strategies compared to unstructured methods.

references.title: Digital Voices of Survival: From Social Media Disclosures to Support Provisions for Domestic Violence Victims
references.abstract: This research presents a computational framework that models domestic violence support-seeking behavior and community support mechanisms, utilizing data from social media communities to inform victim-centered digital interventions.

references.title: Designing Chatbots to Support Victims and Survivors of Domestic Abuse
references.abstract: This study investigates the role of chatbots in supporting victims and survivors of domestic abuse, outlining design considerations and practices to ensure safety, privacy, and emotional support in chatbot interactions.

references.title: How the Internet Facilitates Adverse Childhood Experiences for Youth Who Self-Identify as in Need of Services
references.abstract: This study examines how the internet serves as both a support and a risk factor for youth involved in child welfare and juvenile justice systems, highlighting the need for tailored support systems to promote safe online spaces and resilience.

references.title: Youth Survivors of Human Trafficking: On Improving Health Care Access and Treatment
references.abstract: This qualitative study explores the healthcare barriers, experiences, and needs of youth survivors of human trafficking, emphasizing the importance of accessible and trauma-informed healthcare services for this vulnerable population.

references.title: 2021 - Senate Bill 596 (Becker, Josh), Greenhouse Gases: Cement Sector: Net-Zero Emissions Strategy (Chaptered)
references.abstract: This document outlines the requirements of Senate Bill 596, which mandates the California Air Resources Board to develop a comprehensive strategy for the cement sector to achieve net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2045, with interim targets for 2035.

references.title: Draft Net-Zero GHG Emissions Strategy for the Cement Sector
references.abstract: This draft strategy, released by the California Air Resources Board, outlines key measures for decarbonizing the cement industry to meet the targets set by Senate Bill 596, including a 40% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions intensity by 2035 and net-zero emissions by 2045.

references.title: Senate Passes Low-Carbon Concrete Bill by Senator Josh Becker
references.abstract: This article reports on the passage of Senate Bill 596, which aims to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from concrete and cement production in California, setting a goal of carbon neutrality by 2045 and a 40% reduction in emissions intensity by 2030.

references.title: California elections officials rejected 122,000 ballots in 2024 election. Here’s why
references.abstract: An analysis of mail ballot rejections during the 2024 election by USC’s Center for Inclusive Democracy found that 59% had a non-matching voter signature, 27% were mailed or arrived after the deadline, and 11% had no signature. The other 3% were dismissed for other unspecified reasons.

references.title: California Secretary Of State: Over 99% Of Mail-In Ballots Accepted In Presidential Election
references.abstract: California election officials announced that 99.4% of more than 15 million mail-in ballots were verified and counted in the November election, a rejection rate notably lower than the March primary even though more than twice as many people voted.

references.title: 100,000 Mail-In Ballots Rejected By CA Election Officials in March Primary
references.abstract: On Monday, election data released by the California Secretary of State’s Office revealed that over 100,000 mail-in ballots in California were rejected in the March presidential primary, 1.5% of the 7 million mail-in ballots sent in.

references.title: 100,000 ballots went uncounted in March primary; San Mateo County had fifth highest ballot rejection rate
references.abstract: More than 100,000 mail-in ballots were rejected by California election officials during the March presidential primary, according to data obtained by The Associated Press that highlights a glaring gap in the state’s effort to ensure every vote is counted.

references.title: Study Finds Young Voters More Likely to Have Mail Ballots Rejected
references.abstract: A 2020 study found the most common reasons mail-in ballots get rejected include: returning them too late, forgetting to sign the ballot envelope and signatures that don’t match the one on file.

references.title: Vote-By-Mail Ballot Rejection Reason Reports - Statewide Elections, 2024
references.abstract: In accordance with Elections Code section 15377, elections officials must provide to the Secretary of State the number of vote-by-mail ballots rejected, categorized according to the reason for the rejection, within 31 days of every election.

references.title: SB 887
references.abstract: California Senate Bill 887 (SB 887) aims to accelerate the planning and approval of new transmission facilities to support the state's clean energy goals, including achieving 100% renewable or zero-carbon electricity by 2045. The bill requires the Public Utilities Commission (PUC) and the California Energy Commission (CEC) to provide long-term forecasts to the California Independent System Operator (CAISO) that extend at least 15 years into the future, focusing on reducing reliance on gas plants due to transmission bottlenecks and aligning with the state's offshore wind strategy. Additionally, SB 887 directs the PUC to ask CAISO to consider approving high-priority, long-lead-time projects during its 2022-23 transmission planning process based on existing information.

references.title: SENATE COMMITTEE ON ENERGY, UTILITIES AND
references.abstract: The Senate Committee on Energy, Utilities and Communications' analysis of SB 887 highlights the urgency of building transmission capacity to meet California's clean energy goals. The analysis emphasizes that major new transmission projects often take 10 years or more to build, and without comparable efforts to expand transmission capacity, the state's clean energy transition could fail to meet its climate objectives. SB 887 aims to ensure that transmission development is tackled in a timely and cost-effective manner, preventing it from becoming a barrier to the state's clean energy transition.

references.title: Understanding California’s Transmission Development Delays and Paths to Reform
references.abstract: This report by the Union of Concerned Scientists examines the delays in California's transmission development and proposes reforms to accelerate the integration of renewable energy resources. It underscores the necessity of expanding transmission capacity to connect renewable energy sources to urban centers and meet the state's clean energy goals. The report also discusses the challenges posed by insufficient transmission capacity, which has led to grid stress and delays in retiring fossil fuel power plants, and recommends strategies to streamline transmission development processes.

references.title: Carbon Emission Flow Tracing: Fast Algorithm and California Grid Study
references.abstract: This study presents a novel and computationally efficient approach for quantifying nodal average and marginal carbon emission rates in power systems, applicable to both AC and DC optimal power flow problems. The approach is validated through simulations of the 8,870-bus realistic California grid using actual CAISO data, providing fine-grained spatiotemporal emission analysis for every California county. The study offers insights into how emissions are spatially distributed under varying system conditions, which is crucial for understanding the impact of transmission infrastructure on carbon emissions.

references.title: California Test System (CATS): A Geographically Accurate Test System based on the California Grid
references.abstract: This paper introduces the California Test System (CATS), a synthetic transmission grid in California designed for power systems policy research without revealing critical energy information. The system combines publicly available geographic data of California's electric infrastructure with invented topology and transmission line parameters that are realistic but not real. CATS is suitable for power flow and policy analyses with geo-referenced applications, including studies related to weather, topography, and socio-economic considerations.

references.title: Optimal transmission expansion minimally reduces decarbonization costs of U.S. electricity
references.abstract: This study analyzes optimal transmission expansion jointly with storage, generation, and hourly operations across the three primary interconnects in the United States. The findings indicate that while transmission expansion offers more benefits in a high-renewable system, it decreases the cost of a 100% clean system by only 4% compared to a plan that relies solely on current transmission. Expanding capacity within existing interconnects can achieve most of these savings, suggesting that transmission expansion alone may not be sufficient to minimize decarbonization costs.

references.title: Reducing transmission expansion by co-optimizing sizing of wind, solar, storage and grid connection capacity
references.abstract: This paper develops functionalities to explore the substitutability of storage for transmission and the optimal capacity and siting decisions of renewable energy and battery resources through 2030 in the Western Interconnection of the United States. The findings indicate that optimizing interconnection capacity and co-locating storage with renewable energy resources can reduce total grid connection and shorter-distance transmission capacity expansion by approximately 10%, highlighting the potential of storage to alleviate transmission expansion needs.

references.title: Introduction
references.abstract: This chapter discusses the significance of mobile homes as a primary source of low-income housing in the U.S., noting that throughout the 1990s, manufactured housing was responsible for 66 percent of the new affordable housing produced in the country.

Claim (state-legislators-discuss-groundbreaking-plan-provide-universal-basic-income-foster-youth.txt / #6): Nearly 65% of transition-age youth in foster care reported having lost their jobs, with almost a quarter (23%) at risk of losing their housing.
Constraints:

references.title: Transition from care: status and outcomes of youth who age out of foster care
references.abstract: This study shows that a significant portion of youth exiting the foster care system face serious difficulty transitioning to life on their own. Many live on the streets, lack the money to meet basic living expenses, fail to maintain regular employment, are involved with the criminal justice system, are unable to obtain health care, and experience early pregnancies. Although youth reported exposure to independent living training while in care, few reported concrete assistance. Multiple placements while in care and less education correlated with more difficult postdischarge functioning. Training, services, positive supportive networks, and job experience in care are associated with more positive adjustments.
references.venue: Child and Adolescent Social Work Journal

references.title: Homelessness during the transition from foster care to adulthood
references.abstract: Youths aging out of foster care are at high risk for becoming homeless during the transition to adulthood. Between 31% and 46% of our study participants had been homeless at least once by age 26 years. Running away while in foster care, greater placement instability, being male, having a history of physical abuse, engaging in more delinquent behaviors, and having symptoms of a mental health disorder were associated with an increase in the relative risk of becoming homeless.

references.title: Effects of individual risk and state housing factors on adverse outcomes in a national sample of youth transitioning out of foster care
references.abstract: Approximately 30% of the variation in each of the 19-year-old outcomes could be attributed to state-level effects. Residence in a state that spent above average of CFCIP budget on housing supports reduced the risk of homelessness and incarceration. Living in a state with a higher proportion of housing-burdened low-income renters significantly increased the risk of substance abuse and child birth. Individual-level risks were significant: racial/ethnic minority, male gender, past risk history, placement instability, child behavioral problems, residence in group home or runaway. Remaining in foster care at age 19 reduced the odds of homelessness, incarceration, and substance abuse.

references.title: Homelessness and Aging Out of Foster Care: A National Comparison of Child Welfare-Involved Adolescents
references.abstract: Youth who aged out of foster care experienced similar rates of literal homelessness as youth investigated by child welfare but never placed out of home. This suggests that aging out of foster care contributes to homelessness risk in emerging adulthood.

references.title: Foster care policy and homelessness among youth transitioning to adulthood from foster care
references.abstract: Approximately 18% of the youth experienced homelessness during this period. The study examines whether policies directed towards youth transitioning to adulthood from foster care, Extended Foster Care (EFC) and Supervised Independent Living Placements (SILPs), reduce the risk of homelessness.
references.venue: Child and Adolescent Social Work Journal
